title: Fitting a foam seat pad to a dining chair
subtitle: Cut, cover, and fix a new upholstered foam seat pad to a wooden dining chair with a solid seat
excerpt: How to add a foam seat pad to a hard wooden dining chair. Covers cutting the foam to the seat shape, cutting the cover fabric with allowance, and stapling the fabric around the foam and over the chair seat or to a plywood base.
sourceNotes: Upholstery: A Beginner's Guide, Alex Law (2010). City and Guilds Level 2 Upholstery. UK traditional upholstery trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Two measurements describe seat foam quality. Density (kg/m³) indicates durability (a higher-density foam holds its shape longer; 35 to 45 kg/m³ is the domestic seating range. ILD (Indentation Load Deflection) indicates softness) the force in pounds needed to compress a 100 mm foam block by 25 per cent. Low ILD (15–20) is very soft; 28–35 ILD is medium and the standard dining-chair range; 40+ ILD is firm and used for floor cushions. Both density and ILD together describe a foam; a high-density, low-ILD foam is both durable and soft.

content.text: A foam seat pad fitted to a hard wooden dining chair turns an uncomfortable seat into an upholstered one. The pad is a piece of
cut to the seat outline, wrapped in cover fabric, and either stapled directly to the seat underside or fixed to a separate plywood base that can be removed.

Lay the chair seat flat on a piece of foam sheet and draw around it with a marker. Cut with an electric carving knife or a long-bladed bread knife using a sawing motion without pressing down (pressing distorts the foam and produces a skewed cut. For a chair with a shaped seat, cut to the shape of the seat surface. Foam thickness: 40 to 50 mm for a firm but comfortable dining seat.

Cut the cover fabric 100 mm larger on all sides than the foam) this gives enough to wrap under the seat and staple. Check the pattern grain runs straight front to back. Iron the fabric before cutting.

Turn the chair upside down. Lay the cover fabric face down, then the foam, then the chair seat. Pull the front edge of the fabric up and staple it to the underside of the seat rail at the centre front. Pull the back edge and staple at the centre back) pull firmly enough to remove all slack but not so hard the fabric distorts. Staple the left and right sides. Work from the centre to the corners, stapling at 30 mm intervals. At each corner, fold the fabric into a mitre (fold in one direction first, then fold the second direction over it) and staple.
Drop-in pad method: separate plywood base

Cut a 9 mm plywood base to the seat shape. Glue the foam to the plywood with spray upholstery adhesive. Wrap and staple the cover fabric to the plywood underside following the same sequence. The finished pad sits on the seat surface and is held in place by the chair back and the sitter's weight. Soft rubber feet on the plywood underside stop it sliding.
